atlas.name: Yellow fever
atlas.subtitle: Atlantic mosquito ecology, slavery, empire, and port-city mortality
atlas.summary: Yellow fever is one of the clearest examples of a pathogen whose historical geography was shaped by maritime movement, tropical mosquito ecology, port infrastructure, and imperial warfare.
atlas.why_it_matters: It lets the atlas show how a pathogen can move with people, water storage, ships, and vector habitat rather than by human travel alone.

origin_claim.label: West and Central African transmission zone
origin_claim.date_or_era: Pre-colonial circulation documented before seventeenth-century Atlantic spread
origin_claim.narrative: The best-supported origin frame places endemic yellow fever ecology in tropical Africa before repeated Atlantic export into Caribbean and American port settings.

spread_routes.narrative: Atlantic slave shipping moved infected people, mosquito habitat, and water-storage ecologies into Caribbean port cities.
Port trade and seasonal mosquito ecology helped yellow fever reach Philadelphia, where it reshaped the political geography of the early United States.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Nineteenth to early twentieth century canal era
spread_routes.narrative: Yellow fever and malaria repeatedly disrupted French canal ambitions before vector science and mosquito control changed the engineering balance.

modern_echoes: Vaccination policy and proof-of-entry rules still make yellow fever one of the few infections where geography directly determines travel documentation.
Outbreak coverage still turns on vector control, urban spillover risk, and whether sylvatic transmission is seeding cities.
framing_traps: Do not write yellow fever as a purely human-travel story; mosquito ecology is central.
Do not flatten African endemicity and New World epidemic history into one undifferentiated narrative.

linked_blog_posts.title: The First American Epidemic: How Yellow Fever Exposed the Fault Lines of the Early Republic
How Mosquitos Killed Napoleon's North American Empire Dream
Big Epidemiology: Disease at the Scale of Civilization

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ. Historical overview of global infectious diseases and geopolitics. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Historical African origin framing and Atlantic spread into the Caribbean, Philadelphia, Haiti, and Panama.
citations.note: Core geography-first secondary source for the atlas narrative.

citations.short_citation: Douam F, Ploss A. Yellow Fever Virus: Knowledge Gaps Impeding the Fight Against an Old Foe. Trends Microbiol. 2018.
citations.claim_supported: African endemic ecology, vector biology, and modern transmission framing.
citations.note: Useful modern review of ecology and transmission.

citations.short_citation: Delaporte F. The history of yellow fever. MIT Press. 1991.
citations.claim_supported: Port-city diffusion and the political consequences of yellow fever in the Atlantic world.
citations.note: Historical context source rather than a modern epidemiologic review.

visual_asset.prompt: Museum-drawer style editorial specimen plate for yellow fever, maritime disease atlas aesthetic, Atlantic shipping traces, mosquito ecology motifs, aged cartographic paper, restrained epidemiology newsroom palette, no fantasy, no cartoon pathogen, no glossy stock-art look.
visual_asset.negative_prompt: sci-fi planet, cartoon virus mascot, fake microscope render, glowing neon, purple gradient app art, childish medical icon set
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial specimen plate for yellow fever showing Atlantic route and mosquito-ecology motifs.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Prioritize Atlantic route mood and mosquito-port ecology over clinical illustration.

atlas.name: Cholera
atlas.subtitle: Bay of Bengal ecology, empire, troop movement, and recurring pandemic waves
atlas.summary: Cholera is the classic map disease for showing how river basins, pilgrimage, military movement, and commercial shipping can convert a local ecology into repeated global pandemic waves.
atlas.why_it_matters: It gives the atlas a clean origin basin, repeated outward routes, and a still-live connection between infrastructure failure and epidemic geography.
atlas.atlas_scope: Nineteenth-century pandemic expansion with modern humanitarian echoes

origin_claim.label: Ganges-Brahmaputra delta / Bay of Bengal
origin_claim.date_or_era: Long-standing endemic zone before nineteenth-century pandemic export
origin_claim.narrative: The strongest consensus places cholera's pandemic source ecology in the Bay of Bengal and adjacent delta systems before colonial trade and troop movement accelerated wider spread.

spread_routes.narrative: British military and commercial movement amplified local outbreaks into subcontinental epidemics.
Trade routes and troop movement carried cholera westward into the Middle East and Europe during the earliest pandemic waves.
By the 1830s cholera had jumped into the Caribbean and Mexico, showing how shipping made a delta-rooted disease transcontinental.

modern_echoes: Modern cholera still concentrates where water and sanitation systems break down under war, flooding, or displacement.
The atlas route logic still maps onto contemporary Yemen, Haiti, and eastern/southern Africa coverage.
framing_traps: Do not reduce cholera to an abstract waterborne disease with no political geography.
Do not treat every outbreak as a brand-new emergence; repeated long-range movement is built into its history.

linked_blog_posts.title: Big Epidemiology: Disease at the Scale of Civilization

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ. Historical overview of global infectious diseases and geopolitics. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Bay of Bengal endemicity and British trade-route expansion into repeated pandemics.

citations.short_citation: Mutreja A, Kim DW, Thomson NR et al. Evidence for several waves of global transmission in the seventh cholera pandemic. Nature. 2011.
citations.claim_supported: Repeated transregional spread and phylogeographic movement across cholera pandemic waves.
citations.note: Strong modern genomic support for long-distance spread.

citations.short_citation: Hu D, Liu B, Feng L et al. Origins of the current seventh cholera pandemic. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2016.
citations.claim_supported: Seventh-pandemic lineage emergence and downstream global spread.
citations.note: Useful for connecting historical and genomic narratives.

visual_asset.prompt: Evidence-board style cholera atlas plate, Bay of Bengal delta, river networks, shipping routes, nineteenth-century epidemic cartography, clean editorial layout, public-health newsroom design, no cartooning.
visual_asset.negative_prompt: fantasy map, pirate cartoon, generic blue medical infographic, shiny dashboard look
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial cholera plate centered on delta, river, and shipping-route geography.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Focus on water systems and imperial routes rather than laboratory motifs.

atlas.name: Measles
atlas.subtitle: Dense settlement, cattle-linked ancestry, and colonial spread into immunologically naive populations
atlas.summary: Measles is a useful atlas pathogen because its deeper origin story is partly reconstructed from molecular-clock work, while its colonial spread story is brutally visible in the Americas and Pacific.
atlas.why_it_matters: It shows the difference between debated deep origin, strong consensus on later spread, and the modern reality that measles outbreaks are usually immunity-gap stories.
atlas.atlas_scope: Ancient divergence with early modern colonial spread

origin_claim.label: West Eurasian dense-settlement zone with cattle-linked morbillivirus ancestry
origin_claim.date_or_era: First millennium BCE to first millennium CE, depending on molecular model
origin_claim.narrative: Measles is generally treated as a human morbillivirus related to rinderpest, but the exact timing of divergence remains model-dependent rather than settled to a single year.

spread_routes.narrative: Measles traveled with successive colonial waves and compounded the mortality burden created by smallpox in the Americas.
Once sustained in colonial networks, measles repeatedly entered island populations with little prior immunity and devastating mortality consequences.

modern_echoes: Contemporary measles still follows travel plus immunity gaps rather than any exotic new route mechanism.
Importation, school spread, and low-coverage communities remain the practical map.
framing_traps: Do not pretend the deep origin date is settled more tightly than the literature supports.
Do not turn modern measles into a mystery pathogen story; the practical issue is usually vaccination coverage.

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ. Historical overview of global infectious diseases and geopolitics. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Colonial spread through the Americas and later maritime dissemination.

citations.short_citation: Düx A, Lequime S, Patrono LV et al. Measles virus and rinderpest virus divergence dated to the sixth century BCE. Science. 2020.
citations.claim_supported: Earlier molecular divergence date and dense-settlement origin framing.

citations.short_citation: Moss WJ. Measles. Lancet. 2017.
citations.claim_supported: Transmission characteristics and modern outbreak interpretation.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: arabnews.jp now foregrounds vaccination or vaccine policy in the story.

visual_asset.prompt: Measles atlas specimen plate with dense-settlement texture, route lines across colonial sea lanes, historical paper collage, epidemiology atlas aesthetic, subtle cattle-lineage cue, not cartoonish.
visual_asset.negative_prompt: smiling characters, clinic poster, purple vaporwave, children illustration
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial measles plate linking dense-settlement history and colonial spread.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Keep the divergence-from-rinderpest cue subtle, not literal.

atlas.name: Mpox
atlas.subtitle: Forest-zone recognition, animal trade jumps, and networked global spread
atlas.summary: Mpox belongs in the atlas because it combines a geographically anchored Central/West African history with abrupt export through wildlife trade and then a very different twenty-first century global transmission pattern.
atlas.why_it_matters: It forces the site to distinguish clades, transport mode, and transmission network rather than collapsing everything into one map pin.
atlas.atlas_scope: Central and West African history with 2003 and 2022 global turning points

origin_claim.label: Central and West African forest-zone circulation
origin_claim.date_or_era: Recognized in humans in 1970, likely circulating earlier in animal reservoirs
origin_claim.narrative: Human recognition begins in the Congo Basin and West Africa, but the deeper animal-reservoir history is less settled than the later export events.

spread_routes.from_label: Congo Basin and West African forest regions
spread_routes.narrative: Distinct clades and long intervals of rural detection complicate the idea of one simple linear mpox geography.

spread_routes.to_label: Texas and Midwestern U.S. pet trade
spread_routes.narrative: Imported rodents seeded infection in prairie dogs sold as pets, producing the first recognized outbreak outside Africa.
Travel-linked introductions from Nigeria were followed by broader global transmission in 2022, especially within sexual networks among men who have sex with men.

modern_echoes: Clade naming, network-specific spread, and vaccine access now matter as much as simple country counts.
The 2022 event changed mpox from a niche tropical-medicine map into a real global surveillance map.
framing_traps: Do not flatten clade I and clade II epidemiology into the same story.
Do not erase Central and West African history when covering the 2022 multinational outbreak.

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ; Wilson ME, Petersen E, Koopmans MP. Historical overview and emerging infections chapters. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Central/West African origins, 2003 animal-trade export, and 2022 global outbreak framing.
citations.note: Combines historical overview and emerging infections synthesis.

citations.short_citation: Croft DR, Sotir MJ, Williams CJ et al. Occupational risks during a monkeypox outbreak, Wisconsin, 2003. Emerg Infect Dis. 2007.
citations.claim_supported: 2003 U.S. outbreak tied to imported African rodents and prairie-dog exposure chains.

citations.short_citation: Bunge EM, Hoet B, Chen L et al. The changing epidemiology of human monkeypox—A potential threat? PLoS Negl Trop Dis. 2022.
citations.claim_supported: Changing epidemiology, human transmission, and geographic expansion beyond historical endemic zones.
citations.note: Broad review before the full scale of the 2022 outbreak.

citations.short_citation: Mitjà O, Ogoina D, Titanji BK et al. Monkeypox. Lancet. 2023.
citations.claim_supported: Modern clade framing, transmission patterns, and global outbreak context.

visual_asset.prompt: Mpox atlas editorial plate, Central and West African forest textures, travel-linked outbreak geometry, dossier board aesthetic, muted earth palette, evidence-focused, no stigma cues.
visual_asset.negative_prompt: sensational skin closeup, warning poster, biohazard slop, flashy app chrome
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial mpox plate showing forest-zone history and later export routes.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Avoid sensational lesion imagery; this should read like a serious atlas.

atlas.name: Avian influenza A(H5N1)
atlas.subtitle: Bird flyways, poultry systems, mammalian spillover, and farm-interface risk
atlas.summary: H5N1 belongs in the atlas because its contemporary geography is not a human epidemic map first; it is a bird-route, farm-system, and spillover map whose human layer remains contingent.
atlas.why_it_matters: It creates a genuinely different map grammar from the classic human-to-human pathogens and foregrounds One Health risk.
atlas.atlas_scope: East and Southeast Asian emergence with global bird and farm spread

origin_claim.label: Southern China and adjacent East/Southeast Asian poultry interfaces
origin_claim.date_or_era: Recognized in humans in 1997, with wider avian circulation already established
origin_claim.narrative: The strongest modern surveillance narrative begins in East and Southeast Asian poultry-wild bird interfaces before later global circulation through migratory and farm networks.

spread_routes.from_label: South China / Hong Kong poultry interface
spread_routes.narrative: Highly pathogenic H5 viruses spread across flyways through wild birds and then seeded outbreaks in poultry and wild carnivores.
Clade 2.3.4.4b produced a much more geographically persistent epizootic across wild birds, poultry, and mammalian spillover hosts.

spread_routes.from_label: North American wild bird and poultry circulation
spread_routes.narrative: The dairy-cattle event marked a major shift from bird-centered concern to sustained attention on mammalian agricultural systems and exposed workers.

modern_echoes: Readers need to know whether a map point represents birds, mammals, farms, or humans; these are not interchangeable layers.
Human-case headlines can mislead if the animal geography has changed far more than the human case count has.
framing_traps: Do not imply sustained human transmission when the evidence is still spillover-heavy.
Do not treat one human case and a continent-wide bird epizootic as the same epidemiologic object.

citations.short_citation: Wilson ME, Petersen E, Koopmans MP. Emerging infections. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: East/Asian origin framing, migratory dissemination, and global bird-plus-mammal spread.

citations.short_citation: Adlhoch C, Fusaro A, Gonzales JL et al. Avian influenza overview April-June 2023. EFSA J. 2023.
citations.claim_supported: Wide avian spread across Europe and beyond.

citations.short_citation: Briand FX, Souchaud F, Pierre I et al. Highly pathogenic Avian Influenza A(H5N1) Clade 2.3.4.4b Virus in Domestic Cat, France, 2022. Emerg Infect Dis. 2023.
citations.claim_supported: Mammalian spillover in Europe during the current clade 2.3.4.4b era.
citations.note: Illustrates the expanded non-avian host picture.

citations.short_citation: Agüero M, Monne I, Sánchez A et al. Highly pathogenic avian influenza A(H5N1) virus infection in farmed minks, Spain, October 2022. Euro Surveill. 2023.
citations.claim_supported: Farmed-mammal spillover and adaptation concern.

citations.short_citation: CDC. H5 Bird Flu: Current Situation Summary. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: U.S. dairy-associated spread and human spillover updates.
citations.note: Official surveillance layer for the modern route.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: CDC MMWR now explicitly uses investigation or monitoring language.

visual_asset.prompt: H5N1 atlas plate with migratory flyway overlays, poultry and dairy interface cues, field-notebook and surveillance-board look, serious public-health editorial style, no mascot birds.
visual_asset.negative_prompt: cute poultry cartoon, disaster movie poster, sci-fi plague orb
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial H5N1 plate emphasizing flyways and farm-interface surveillance.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Keep it more surveillance map than dramatic outbreak art.

atlas.name: Hantaviruses
atlas.subtitle: Rodent-host lineages, hemorrhagic fever versus cardiopulmonary syndromes, and one crucial Andes-virus exception
atlas.summary: Hantaviruses belong in the atlas because they break the habit of pretending every pathogen has one clean human-travel route. The real map is rodent-host biogeography, ecological exposure settings, and the split between Old World hemorrhagic-fever viruses and New World cardiopulmonary viruses.
atlas.why_it_matters: It forces the atlas to behave like an encyclopedia rather than a single-route animation and keeps us honest about host ecology, geography, and syndrome differences.
atlas.atlas_scope: Family-level rodent-host geography with variant-specific human disease lenses

origin_claim.label: Eurasian and American rodent-host lineages
origin_claim.date_or_era: Twentieth-century human recognition layered on much older rodent-virus evolution
origin_claim.narrative: There is no honest single origin pin for all hantaviruses. The stronger atlas frame is family-level rodent-host evolution across Eurasia and the Americas, then variant-specific human syndromes with distinct geographic signatures.

spread_routes.to_label: Korea, China, and the Russian Far East
spread_routes.date_or_era: Twentieth-century recognition of hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
spread_routes.narrative: The classical Old World hantavirus story is not a traveler chain but a host-and-landscape pattern tied to murid rodents and hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome.

spread_routes.from_label: North and South American sigmodontine rodent zones
spread_routes.to_label: Four Corners and southern cone exposure settings
spread_routes.date_or_era: 1990s emergence framing and later regional expansion
spread_routes.narrative: New World hantaviruses became a major public story after cardiopulmonary syndromes were recognized in the Americas, but the real map remains rodent-host geography plus human disturbance and exposure.

modern_echoes: Travelers, field workers, rural residents, and military trainees usually encounter hantavirus risk through disturbed rodent habitat rather than crowded metropolitan spread.
The one question that changes media framing fast is whether the story points to Andes virus or another person-to-person concern rather than a standard rodent-exposure event.
framing_traps: Do not write all hantaviruses as though they share one syndrome, one rodent host, or one transmission pattern.
Do not let the Andes-virus exception overwrite the much larger family-level geography.

linked_blog_posts.title: Hantavirus at Sea: What We Know About the MV Hondius Outbreak (The Pathogen Dispatch #2)

citations.short_citation: Jonsson CB, Figueiredo LTM, Vapalahti O. A global perspective on hantavirus ecology, epidemiology, and disease. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2010.
citations.claim_supported: Family-level hantavirus ecology, rodent-host associations, and the division between Old World and New World disease patterns.
citations.note: Best broad synthesis source for a family-level atlas entry.

citations.short_citation: Hjelle B, Torres-Pérez F. Hantaviruses in the Americas and their role as emerging pathogens. Viruses. 2010.
citations.claim_supported: American hantavirus ecology, cardiopulmonary syndromes, and host-geography framing.
citations.note: Broad review of hantavirus emergence in the Americas.

citations.short_citation: Bi Z, Formenty PBH, Roth CE. Hantavirus infection: A review and global update. J Infect Dev Ctries. 2008.
citations.claim_supported: Global hantavirus distribution and the Old World hemorrhagic-fever geography.
citations.note: Useful bridge between HFRS and broader global distribution.

citations.short_citation: Martínez-Valdebenito C, Calvo M, Vial C et al. Person-to-person household and nosocomial transmission of Andes hantavirus, southern Chile, 2011. Emerg Infect Dis. 2014.
citations.claim_supported: Andes-virus person-to-person transmission as the key exception.
citations.note: Specific evidence for the atlas caution layer.

citations.short_citation: Douglas KO, Payne K, Sabino-Santos G et al. Influence of climatic factors on human hantavirus infections in Latin America and the Caribbean: A systematic review. Pathogens. 2022.
citations.claim_supported: Climate and landscape conditions affecting hantavirus geography in Latin America and the Caribbean.

variants.name: Hantaan orthohantavirus
variants.subtitle: Old World hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome centered on Korean and northeast Asian rodent ecologies
variants.summary: Hantaan virus is the classic Old World hantavirus frame and the right default for an encyclopedia view because it grounds the family in the hemorrhagic-fever geography that defined early recognition.
variants.why_it_matters: It restores the older and broader hantavirus story that gets erased when modern news cycles jump straight to Andes virus.
variants.atlas_scope: Family-level rodent-host geography with variant-specific human disease lenses

origin_claim.label: Korean peninsula and adjacent northeast Asian rodent zones
origin_claim.date_or_era: Recognized during the Korean War, with older endemic circulation
origin_claim.narrative: Hantaan virus is tied to striped field-mouse ecologies in Korea, China, and nearby parts of Eurasia and became globally legible during Korean War hemorrhagic-fever outbreaks.

spread_routes.narrative: The decisive map for Hantaan virus is rodent habitat intersecting with soldiers, farmers, and disturbed field settings rather than civilian travel chains.
The wider HFRS map extends across East Asia where related rodent hosts and agricultural exposure sustain recurring transmission.

modern_echoes: HFRS reporting still turns on seasonality, rodent exposure, and occupational setting more than cross-border passenger travel.
A family-level atlas needs this variant because it anchors hantavirus outside the news-driven Americas frame.
framing_traps: Do not collapse HFRS and cardiopulmonary syndromes into one undifferentiated clinical map.
Do not start the family story in South America just because Andes virus is narratively dramatic.

linked_blog_posts.title: Hantavirus at Sea: What We Know About the MV Hondius Outbreak (The Pathogen Dispatch #2)

citations.short_citation: Lee HW, Lee PW, Johnson KM. Isolation of the etiologic agent of Korean hemorrhagic fever. J Infect Dis. 1978.
citations.claim_supported: Recognition of Hantaan virus as the etiologic agent of Korean hemorrhagic fever.

citations.short_citation: Bi Z, Formenty PBH, Roth CE. Hantavirus infection: A review and global update. J Infect Dev Ctries. 2008.
citations.claim_supported: East Asian HFRS geography and broader Old World distribution.

visual_asset.prompt: Hantavirus atlas plate with Andean landscape textures, rodent-ecology cues, cabin and rural exposure motifs, scientific editorial collage, restrained and eerie, not horror.
visual_asset.negative_prompt: horror movie poster, dripping blood, cartoon rat, glossy medical brochure
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial hantavirus plate focused on Andean ecology and exposure settings.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Emphasize ecology and uncertainty instead of fear-bait.

variants.name: Sin Nombre orthohantavirus
variants.subtitle: Four Corners emergence, deer-mouse ecology, and the New World cardiopulmonary turn
variants.summary: Sin Nombre virus changed how many readers understand hantaviruses by making a rodent-borne ecological disease look suddenly emergent in the American Southwest.
variants.why_it_matters: It explains why U.S. readers often think hantavirus starts in the Four Corners when that is really the start of one major New World recognition event.
variants.atlas_scope: Family-level rodent-host geography with variant-specific human disease lenses

origin_claim.label: Deer-mouse ecologies in the U.S. Southwest
origin_claim.date_or_era: Recognized in 1993, with older enzootic circulation
origin_claim.narrative: Sin Nombre virus was recognized after the Four Corners outbreak, but the ecology was already present in deer-mouse populations before the human cluster made it visible.

spread_routes.to_label: Four Corners household and peridomestic exposure settings
spread_routes.narrative: The famous Four Corners outbreak reflects rodent population dynamics, human dwellings, and aerosolized exposure during cleanup rather than human-to-human spread.

spread_routes.to_label: Wider western North American risk zone
spread_routes.narrative: Once recognized, related western U.S. exposures showed that the disease map followed reservoir ecology and building conditions more than any passenger network.

modern_echoes: Cabin cleanup, rodent infestation, and environmental disturbance remain the operative map categories.
Climate-linked rodent booms still matter more than international travel in most Sin Nombre reporting.
framing_traps: Do not call Sin Nombre a mysterious new spillover just because the 1993 cluster was the first dramatic public recognition.
Do not imply routine person-to-person spread.

linked_blog_posts.title: Hantavirus at Sea: What We Know About the MV Hondius Outbreak (The Pathogen Dispatch #2)

citations.short_citation: Nichol ST, Spiropoulou CF, Morzunov S et al. Genetic identification of a hantavirus associated with an outbreak of acute respiratory illness. Science. 1993.
citations.claim_supported: Identification of the Four Corners-associated hantavirus and cardiopulmonary outbreak framing.

citations.short_citation: Hjelle B, Torres-Pérez F. Hantaviruses in the Americas and their role as emerging pathogens. Viruses. 2010.
citations.claim_supported: North American ecology and New World hantavirus patterns.

visual_asset.negative_prompt: horror movie poster, dripping blood, cartoon rat, glossy medical brochure
visual_asset.alt_text: Editorial hantavirus plate focused on Andean ecology and exposure settings.
visual_asset.editorial_note: Emphasize ecology and uncertainty instead of fear-bait.

variants.name: Andes orthohantavirus
variants.subtitle: Southern cone rodent ecologies and the major person-to-person exception inside the hantavirus family
variants.summary: Andes virus matters because it is the exception journalists remember, not the family baseline they should begin with.
variants.why_it_matters: It is the variant that turns a rodent-exposure story into a transmission-story question.
variants.atlas_scope: Family-level rodent-host geography with variant-specific human disease lenses

origin_claim.label: Southern Andes rodent ecologies
origin_claim.date_or_era: Late twentieth century recognition with older enzootic circulation
origin_claim.narrative: Andes virus is associated with southern South American rodent ecologies and gained outsized attention because of evidence for person-to-person transmission in specific settings.

spread_routes.narrative: Andes virus clusters in southern South America remain the most important person-to-person exception within hantavirus reporting.
The practical route is still rodent-linked exposure, but once Andes virus is implicated the reporting stakes shift toward cluster transmission and close-contact investigation.

modern_echoes: This is the strain that most readily turns a countryside exposure story into an urgent transmission story.
Travel and expedition reporting tends to overfocus on Andes virus precisely because it is the exception.
framing_traps: Do not project Andes-virus person-to-person concerns onto the rest of the family.
Do not forget that rodent ecology still sits underneath the exceptional transmission narrative.

linked_blog_posts.title: Hantavirus at Sea: What We Know About the MV Hondius Outbreak (The Pathogen Dispatch #2)

citations.claim_supported: Andes-virus person-to-person transmission and southern cone cluster interpretation.
citations.note: Key exception paper for the family.

atlas.name: Dengue
atlas.subtitle: Aedes routes, urbanization, and shipping-enabled vector expansion
atlas.summary: Dengue makes the atlas useful for modern readers because it shows how vector range expansion, used-tire shipping, urbanization, and travel can redraw the map without requiring a single ancient origin story.
atlas.why_it_matters: It turns the atlas toward contemporary global change instead of leaving it stranded in historical imperial routes.
atlas.atlas_scope: Asian origin framing with modern vector-driven global expansion

origin_claim.label: South-East Asian and western Pacific transmission zone
origin_claim.date_or_era: Modern endemic framing consolidated in twentieth-century tropical Asia
origin_claim.narrative: Dengue's deeper origin is more diffuse than yellow fever's, but the strongest modern atlas frame places sustained amplification in tropical Asia before wide contemporary global expansion.

spread_routes.to_label: Indian Ocean and South Asia circuits
spread_routes.narrative: Rapid urbanization and dense Aedes-human contact zones helped dengue expand across connected Asian cities.
Global traffic and Aedes expansion turned dengue into a recurring hemispheric travel-plus-vector story.
The spread of Aedes albopictus through shipping and the used-tire trade is a core modern map mechanism for dengue risk expansion.

modern_echoes: A reader looking at southern Europe, Oman, or the southern United States is really looking at vector geography, not just imported human cases.
Dengue maps are increasingly climate and infrastructure maps.
framing_traps: Do not confuse imported cases with sustained local transmission.
Do not write dengue as though the virus moves independently of the mosquito layer.

citations.short_citation: Wilson ME, Petersen E, Koopmans MP; Khatib AN, Matsee W, Semenza JC. Emerging infections and climate change chapters. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Global Aedes expansion, shipping, urbanization, and climate-linked spread.
citations.note: Combines the emerging infections and climate chapters.

citations.short_citation: Kraemer MUG, Reiner RC Jr, Brady OJ et al. Past and future spread of the arbovirus vectors Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus. Nat Microbiol. 2019.
citations.claim_supported: Vector spread and the transport routes that underpin dengue range expansion.
citations.note: Vector map paper more than a virus paper, which is exactly why it belongs here.

citations.short_citation: Messina JP, Brady OJ, Golding N et al. The current and future global distribution and population at risk of dengue. Nat Microbiol. 2019.
citations.claim_supported: Modern geographic spread and population-at-risk framing.
citations.note: Strong for the modern echo layer.

atlas.name: Malaria
atlas.subtitle: Anopheles ecologies, empire, labor, and the long retreat and return of a mosquito-borne world disease
atlas.summary: Malaria belongs in the atlas because it shows how vector ecology, land use, labor systems, altitude, war, and climate can redraw disease geography across centuries without one neat origin story doing all the work.
atlas.why_it_matters: It keeps the atlas from becoming virus-only and forces a map grammar built around mosquitoes, species differences, and ecological receptivity.
atlas.atlas_scope: Ancient African-rooted burden with colonial and modern climate-vector redistribution

origin_claim.label: Deep African and Old World malaria ecologies
origin_claim.date_or_era: Ancient human-parasite history with later species-specific dispersal
origin_claim.narrative: The broadest consensus places the deepest burden and evolutionary importance of falciparum malaria in Africa, while the wider human malaria story includes multiple Plasmodium species with different dispersal histories.

spread_routes.narrative: Enslavement, shipping, and mosquito-suitable plantation ecologies helped entrench malaria across the Atlantic world.
Malaria repeatedly advanced or retreated with irrigation, settlement, transport, and temperature, making altitude and land-use change central to its map.

spread_routes.from_label: Endemic African and Asian transmission zones
spread_routes.to_label: Europe and North America reintroduction risk
spread_routes.narrative: Modern malaria news often turns on imported cases, airport malaria, or local receptivity where competent Anopheles mosquitoes still exist.

modern_echoes: Malaria maps are always partly maps of vectors, prophylaxis, and health-system reach rather than just case counts.
The contemporary question is often reintroduction or shifting suitability, not simply ancient endemic burden.
framing_traps: Do not write malaria as one organism with one stable global route.
Do not confuse imported malaria with reestablished local transmission.

linked_blog_posts.title: Disease in the Early Colonies: Pre-Revolutionary War Disease Ecology and Outbreaks
Humanity’s Deadliest Companions: Checking the Till on the Deadliest Microbes in History

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ; Wilson ME, Petersen E, Koopmans MP; Khatib AN, Matsee W, Semenza JC. Historical overview, emerging infections, and climate change chapters. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Historical global spread, colonial and travel-linked malaria geography, and climate-linked transmission shifts.

citations.short_citation: Carter R, Mendis KN. Evolutionary and historical aspects of the burden of malaria. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2002.
citations.claim_supported: Deep historical burden and long-run geographic framing of malaria.

citations.short_citation: Van den Ende J, Lynen L, Elsen P et al. A cluster of airport malaria in Belgium in 1995. Acta Clin Belg. 1998.
citations.claim_supported: Reintroduction and airport-malaria framing in nonendemic settings.
citations.note: Useful for modern travel-linked route interpretation.

citations.short_citation: Mordecai EA, Ryan SJ, Caldwell JM et al. Climate change could shift disease burden from malaria to arboviruses in Africa. Lancet Planet Health. 2020.
citations.claim_supported: Climatic suitability shifts and future geographic redistribution.

atlas.name: Tuberculosis
atlas.subtitle: Human coexpansion, crowding, industry, migration, and the stubborn durability of airborne inequality
atlas.summary: Tuberculosis belongs in the atlas because it is both ancient and modern at once: a human co-traveler that thrives in crowding, poverty, institutional confinement, and uneven public health capacity.
atlas.why_it_matters: It turns the atlas toward chronic structural transmission instead of one spectacular frontier jump.
atlas.atlas_scope: Deep human coexpansion with industrial, colonial, and migration-linked redistribution

origin_claim.label: African human-associated origin with later Eurasian and global coexpansion
origin_claim.date_or_era: Deep premodern origin with Neolithic and later human dispersal
origin_claim.narrative: Modern genomic work supports an African origin for the MTBC followed by dispersal with human populations, but the later epidemiologic story is built in cities, mines, barracks, prisons, and migration corridors.

spread_routes.narrative: Tuberculosis did not need empire to spread the first time; it rode with human movement and settlement long before modern surveillance existed.

spread_routes.to_label: Industrial European and North American cities
spread_routes.narrative: The white-plague map is a crowding map: factories, tenements, prisons, workhouses, and other poorly ventilated spaces turned tuberculosis into a signature disease of industrial inequality.

spread_routes.to_label: Europe and North America migration and screening corridors
spread_routes.narrative: Modern tuberculosis geography is shaped by migration, HIV, drug resistance, and whether receiving health systems can diagnose and sustain treatment.

modern_echoes: A TB map is usually a map of crowding, incarceration, poverty, migration screening, and treatment failure rather than a map of dramatic emergence.
MDR and XDR TB turn ordinary travel and migration corridors into policy flashpoints.
framing_traps: Do not write tuberculosis as a disease of the past just because its nineteenth-century urban iconography is familiar.
Do not reduce TB geography to migrants while ignoring prisons, mines, homelessness, and weak treatment systems.

linked_blog_posts.title: The White Plague Returns?: Tuberculosis and the Fragility of Public Health (The Pathogen Dispatch)

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ. Historical overview of global infectious diseases and geopolitics. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Historical burden, industrial-era spread, and geopolitical significance of tuberculosis.

citations.short_citation: Comas I, Coscolla M, Luo T et al. Out-of-Africa migration and Neolithic coexpansion of Mycobacterium tuberculosis with modern humans. Nat Genet. 2013.
citations.claim_supported: Deep origin and coexpansion framework for MTBC.

citations.short_citation: Hargreaves S, Lönnroth K, Nellums LB et al. Multidrug-resistant tuberculosis and migration to Europe. Clin Microbiol Infect. 2017.
citations.claim_supported: Migration-linked modern European TB policy and MDR concerns.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Global tuberculosis report 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Contemporary burden and global control context.

citations.short_citation: Dubos R, Dubos J. The White Plague: Tuberculosis, Man, and Society. Rutgers University Press. 1952.
citations.claim_supported: Industrial-era urban tuberculosis and social framing.
citations.note: Historical context source for the white-plague period.

atlas.name: Plague
atlas.subtitle: Steppe reservoirs, siege and shipping, and the repeated collision of rodents, fleas, and empire
atlas.summary: Plague belongs in the atlas because it is one of the clearest historical demonstrations that reservoir ecology, trade corridors, warfare, and maritime ports can repeatedly rewire a continent.
atlas.why_it_matters: It lets the atlas bridge deep historical fear, genomic reconstruction, and the very practical map logic of rodents, fleas, and ports.
atlas.atlas_scope: Central Asian reservoir framing with Justinianic, Black Death, and third-pandemic routes

origin_claim.label: Central Asian rodent-reservoir zone
origin_claim.date_or_era: Deep zoonotic reservoir history with repeated later human pandemic spillovers
origin_claim.narrative: The strongest broad frame places plague in Central Asian rodent reservoirs, with later eruptions into human trade and military networks rather than one single human-origin event.

spread_routes.from_label: Central Asian reservoir and caravan zones
spread_routes.to_label: Crimea and Black Sea trade ports
spread_routes.narrative: Plague moved westward through overland and maritime trade interfaces before exploding into Mediterranean port cities.
The Black Death route remains the most famous plague map: port to port, ship to city, then inland through dense human settlement.

spread_routes.to_label: Bombay, San Francisco, Sydney, and other global ports
spread_routes.date_or_era: Late nineteenth to early twentieth century third pandemic
spread_routes.narrative: The third pandemic made plague legible as a modern port disease moving through rats, ships, and urban infrastructure.

modern_echoes: Plague still matters because reservoir ecology in places like Madagascar and the western United States can still produce real outbreaks.
The modern lesson is less apocalypse than the durability of zoonotic reservoirs and infrastructural vulnerability.
framing_traps: Do not write every historical plague narrative as equally well established; the genomic and documentary evidence is uneven across pandemics.
Do not turn plague into pure medieval theater and ignore reservoir ecology.

linked_blog_posts.title: Did the Vikings Bring Plague Across the North Sea? Norse Voyages, Infectious Disease, and the North Atlantic

citations.short_citation: Tuan JJ, Cox FEG, Bia FJ. Historical overview of global infectious diseases and geopolitics. In Routledge Handbook of Infectious Diseases: A Geographical Guide. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Justinianic plague, Black Death, Greenland, and the later port-disease framing.

citations.short_citation: Stenseth NC, Atshabar BB, Begon M et al. Plague: Past, present, and future. PLoS Med. 2008.
citations.claim_supported: Reservoir ecology, transmission mechanisms, and long-run plague history.

citations.short_citation: Bos KI, Schuenemann VJ, Golding GB et al. A draft genome of Yersinia pestis from victims of the Black Death. Nature. 2011.
citations.claim_supported: Genomic confirmation and modern reconstruction of Black Death plague.

citations.short_citation: Frith J. The history of plague - Part 1. The three great pandemics. J Mil Vet Health. 2012.
citations.claim_supported: Third-pandemic port spread and the broader narrative of recurring plague waves.
citations.note: Useful summary source for the later port era.
